militaryActions.title: Dominican Republic Intervention
militaryActions.summary: In 1965, Johnson ordered U.S. troops to intervene in the Dominican Republic to prevent what he claimed was a communist takeover during a civil conflict. The intervention involved more than 20,000 troops and resulted in the establishment of an interim government. Critics argued that Johnson exaggerated the communist threat. The operation reflected Johnson’s willingness to use military force to maintain stability in the Western Hemisphere. It also demonstrated the global reach of Cold War containment.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Expanded Development Aid to Latin America and Asia
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Johnson expanded foreign-aid programs to support economic development and counter communist influence. His administration increased funding for education, agriculture, and infrastructure in developing nations. Johnson believed that poverty and instability created openings for communist movements. Aid programs complemented military strategies in Vietnam and diplomatic efforts in Latin America. Although results were mixed, the initiatives reflected Johnson’s belief in development as a tool of foreign policy. They also deepened U.S. involvement in global affairs.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Gulf of Tonkin Resolution
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: After reports of attacks on U.S. ships in the Gulf of Tonkin, Johnson secured congressional authorization to use military force in Southeast Asia. The resolution granted broad powers to conduct military operations without a formal declaration of war. Johnson used it as the legal basis for escalating the Vietnam War. Later investigations revealed that the second reported attack may not have occurred. The resolution became a symbol of unchecked executive power. It shaped debates over war powers for decades.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Six-Day War Diplomacy
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: During the 1967 Six-Day War, Johnson attempted to balance support for Israel with broader regional stability. His administration sought to prevent Soviet intervention while maintaining relations with Arab states. Johnson emphasized diplomatic restraint and called for a negotiated settlement. The war reshaped Middle Eastern geopolitics and U.S. involvement in the region. Johnson’s response reflected Cold War calculations as well as domestic political pressures. The conflict set the stage for deeper U.S. engagement in Middle Eastern affairs.

treatiesNegotiated.title: SALT I Treaty
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Nixon negotiated the Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT I) with the Soviet Union in 1972. The treaty limited the number of intercontinental ballistic missiles and anti-ballistic missile systems. SALT I marked the first major arms-control agreement of the Cold War. It reflected Nixon’s strategy of détente, aimed at reducing tensions with the Soviet Union. The agreement helped stabilize the nuclear balance. It laid the foundation for future arms-control negotiations.

militaryActions.title: Cambodia and Laos Bombing Campaigns
militaryActions.summary: Nixon expanded the Vietnam War into Cambodia and Laos through secret and later public bombing campaigns. He argued that North Vietnamese forces used these countries as sanctuaries. The bombings aimed to pressure North Vietnam into negotiations. Critics condemned the operations as illegal and destabilizing. The campaigns contributed to political upheaval in Cambodia. They remain among the most controversial actions of the Nixon presidency.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Vietnamization Support Programs
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Nixon’s Vietnamization policy sought to shift the burden of fighting to South Vietnamese forces while reducing U.S. troop levels. The administration provided extensive military and economic aid to strengthen South Vietnam’s government and army. Nixon argued that Vietnamization would allow the United States to withdraw honorably. The policy had mixed results, as South Vietnamese forces struggled to operate independently. Vietnamization shaped the final years of the war and U.S. foreign-aid strategy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Opening to China
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Nixon dramatically shifted global geopolitics by opening diplomatic relations with the People’s Republic of China. His 1972 visit to Beijing ended decades of isolation and hostility. Nixon sought to exploit the Sino-Soviet split and strengthen the U.S. position in the Cold War. The opening paved the way for expanded trade and cultural exchange. It remains one of the most significant diplomatic breakthroughs in U.S. history. The move reshaped global power dynamics.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Détente with the Soviet Union
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Nixon pursued détente to reduce tensions with the Soviet Union through diplomacy, arms control, and expanded trade. He met with Soviet leaders in multiple summits and signed agreements on scientific cooperation and nuclear safety. Détente marked a shift from confrontation to negotiation. Critics argued that it conceded too much to the Soviets. Supporters viewed it as essential to preventing nuclear war. The policy defined U.S.–Soviet relations in the 1970s.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Helsinki Accords
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Ford signed the Helsinki Accords in 1975, a major diplomatic agreement between the United States, the Soviet Union, and European nations. The accords recognized post–World War II borders in Europe and included commitments to human rights. Although not legally binding, the agreement strengthened dissident movements in Eastern Europe. Critics accused Ford of legitimizing Soviet control over Eastern Europe. Supporters argued that the human-rights provisions undermined Soviet authority. The accords became a cornerstone of Cold War diplomacy.

militaryActions.title: Mayagüez Incident
militaryActions.summary: In 1975, Cambodian forces seized the U.S. merchant ship SS Mayagüez. Ford ordered a military rescue operation, which successfully recovered the crew but resulted in significant U.S. casualties. The incident occurred shortly after the fall of Saigon and was seen as a test of American resolve. Ford framed the operation as necessary to defend U.S. citizens and uphold international law. The mission remains controversial due to planning failures and unclear intelligence. It was one of the last military actions of the Vietnam era.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: End of the Vietnam War
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Ford presided over the final evacuation of U.S. personnel from Saigon in 1975 as North Vietnamese forces captured the city. The fall of Saigon marked the end of the Vietnam War. Ford requested additional aid for South Vietnam, but Congress refused. The evacuation symbolized the limits of U.S. power and the consequences of prolonged conflict. Ford emphasized healing and rebuilding alliances after the war. The episode reshaped American foreign-policy thinking.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Camp David Accords
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Carter brokered the Camp David Accords between Egypt and Israel in 1978. The agreements led to the 1979 Egypt–Israel Peace Treaty, the first peace agreement between Israel and an Arab state. Carter invested significant personal effort in the negotiations. The accords reshaped Middle Eastern diplomacy and reduced the likelihood of major regional war. They remain one of the most significant diplomatic achievements in U.S. history.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Panama Canal Treaties
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Carter negotiated treaties with Panama that transferred control of the Panama Canal to Panama by the end of 1999. The agreements aimed to improve relations with Latin America and address long-standing grievances. Critics argued that the treaties weakened U.S. strategic interests. Supporters viewed them as essential to respecting national sovereignty. The treaties remain a major milestone in U.S.–Latin American relations.

militaryActions.title: Operation Eagle Claw
militaryActions.summary: Carter authorized Operation Eagle Claw in 1980 to rescue American hostages held in Tehran. The mission failed due to mechanical problems and a fatal collision between aircraft. The failure damaged Carter’s credibility and contributed to perceptions of American weakness. Carter accepted responsibility for the operation. The episode highlighted the challenges of special operations and interagency coordination. It influenced later reforms in U.S. military command structures.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Human Rights–Focused Aid Programs
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Carter reoriented U.S. foreign aid to emphasize human rights, conditioning assistance on democratic reforms and respect for civil liberties. His administration reduced aid to authoritarian regimes and increased support for human-rights organizations. Carter believed that moral leadership strengthened U.S. credibility. The policy reshaped global perceptions of American foreign policy. It also created tensions with Cold War strategic priorities.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Iran Hostage Crisis
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: In 1979, Iranian revolutionaries seized the U.S. embassy in Tehran and held 52 Americans hostage for 444 days. The crisis dominated Carter’s presidency and strained U.S.–Iran relations. Carter imposed economic sanctions and pursued diplomatic negotiations. The hostages were released on the day Carter left office. The crisis reshaped U.S. policy in the Middle East. It remains one of the most defining events of the late Cold War.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Soviet Invasion of Afghanistan Response
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: After the Soviet Union invaded Afghanistan in 1979, Carter condemned the action as a grave threat to global stability. He imposed economic sanctions, boycotted the 1980 Moscow Olympics, and increased military aid to anti-Soviet forces. Carter also announced the Carter Doctrine, declaring that the United States would use military force to defend its interests in the Persian Gulf. The invasion marked a major escalation of Cold War tensions. Carter’s response reshaped U.S. strategy in the Middle East.

treatiesNegotiated.title: INF Treaty
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Reagan negotiated the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty with the Soviet Union in 1987, eliminating an entire class of nuclear missiles and establishing unprecedented verification measures. The treaty marked a major breakthrough in Cold War arms control and symbolized a dramatic shift from Reagan’s earlier confrontational posture. It strengthened U.S.–Soviet relations and contributed directly to the de-escalation of Cold War tensions. The agreement demonstrated Reagan’s willingness to combine military strength with diplomatic engagement. It remains one of the most significant arms-control achievements of the 20th century.

militaryActions.title: Invasion of Grenada
militaryActions.summary: Reagan ordered the invasion of Grenada in 1983 after a Marxist coup threatened regional stability and endangered American medical students. The operation, known as Operation Urgent Fury, quickly toppled the government and installed a pro-Western administration. Reagan framed the invasion as necessary to counter Soviet and Cuban influence in the Caribbean. Critics argued that it violated international law and bypassed Congress. The mission boosted Reagan’s domestic popularity and demonstrated U.S. willingness to use force during the Cold War.

militaryActions.title: Bombing of Libya
militaryActions.summary: In 1986, Reagan ordered airstrikes against Libya in response to terrorist attacks linked to Muammar Gaddafi’s regime, including the bombing of a Berlin nightclub frequented by U.S. servicemen. The operation targeted military facilities in Tripoli and Benghazi. Reagan argued that the strikes were necessary to deter future terrorism and demonstrate American resolve. The attack heightened tensions with Libya and drew international criticism. It marked a major moment in the evolution of U.S. counterterrorism policy.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Support for Anti-Communist Movements
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Reagan expanded foreign-aid programs to support anti-communist movements worldwide, including the Contras in Nicaragua, the mujahideen in Afghanistan, and anti-Soviet forces in Africa and Asia. These programs combined covert CIA operations with overt military and economic assistance. Reagan framed the support as part of a global struggle between freedom and totalitarianism. Critics argued that some U.S.-backed groups committed human-rights abuses and destabilized regions. The aid programs played a major role in Cold War proxy conflicts and contributed to the eventual collapse of Soviet influence.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Reagan Doctrine
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: The Reagan Doctrine committed the United States to supporting anti-communist insurgencies around the world. It represented a major expansion of Cold War strategy beyond containment toward rollback of Soviet influence. The doctrine guided U.S. involvement in conflicts in Afghanistan, Nicaragua, Angola, and Cambodia. Reagan argued that aiding freedom fighters would weaken the Soviet Union and promote democratic values. Critics warned that the policy fueled proxy wars and empowered extremist groups. The doctrine significantly shaped global geopolitics in the 1980s.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Reagan announced the Strategic Defense Initiative in 1983, proposing a space-based missile defense system to protect the United States from nuclear attack. Critics dubbed it 'Star Wars' and questioned its feasibility and cost. Reagan believed SDI would render nuclear weapons obsolete and shift the strategic balance. The initiative pressured the Soviet Union economically and technologically. Although never fully realized, SDI influenced arms-control negotiations and contributed to the Soviet Union’s strategic reassessment.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: End of the Cold War Foundations
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Reagan’s second-term diplomacy with Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev helped lay the groundwork for the end of the Cold War. Through a series of summits, the two leaders negotiated arms-control agreements, reduced tensions, and improved communication. Reagan shifted from early confrontational rhetoric to constructive engagement. His combination of military buildup and diplomatic outreach reshaped U.S.–Soviet relations. The thaw in relations accelerated reforms within the Soviet Union and contributed to the Cold War’s peaceful conclusion.

treatiesNegotiated.title: START I Treaty
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Bush negotiated and signed the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START I) with the Soviet Union in 1991. The treaty mandated deep cuts in strategic nuclear arsenals and established extensive verification mechanisms. START I marked the most significant nuclear arms reduction agreement to date. It reflected Bush’s commitment to managing the end of the Cold War responsibly. The treaty helped stabilize U.S.–Soviet relations during a period of dramatic geopolitical change.

militaryActions.title: Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm)
militaryActions.summary: Bush led a multinational coalition to expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait after Saddam Hussein’s 1990 invasion. The operation involved massive airstrikes followed by a swift ground campaign. Bush secured United Nations authorization and broad international support. The war demonstrated overwhelming U.S. military superiority and reaffirmed American leadership in the post–Cold War world. Bush chose not to overthrow Saddam Hussein, arguing that doing so would destabilize the region. The conflict reshaped Middle Eastern geopolitics.

militaryActions.title: Operation Just Cause (Panama)
militaryActions.summary: Bush ordered the invasion of Panama in 1989 to remove dictator Manuel Noriega, who was involved in drug trafficking and had annulled democratic elections. The operation quickly toppled Noriega’s regime and restored the elected government. Bush justified the intervention as necessary to protect American citizens, uphold democracy, and combat narcotics trafficking. Critics questioned the legality of the invasion. The operation demonstrated Bush’s willingness to use force in the Western Hemisphere.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Post–Cold War Assistance to Eastern Europe
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Bush expanded economic aid to Eastern European nations transitioning from communism to democracy. Programs supported market reforms, privatization, and political liberalization. Bush believed that successful transitions would stabilize Europe and prevent a resurgence of authoritarianism. Aid programs strengthened U.S. influence in the region. They also helped integrate former communist states into Western institutions.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: End of the Cold War
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Bush presided over the peaceful end of the Cold War as the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991. He worked closely with Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev to manage the transition and avoid instability. Bush emphasized caution and avoided triumphalism, believing that stability was essential. His diplomacy helped ensure that the dissolution of the Soviet Union did not lead to widespread conflict. The end of the Cold War reshaped global politics and U.S. foreign policy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Reunification of Germany
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Bush strongly supported the reunification of East and West Germany after the fall of the Berlin Wall. He worked with European allies and the Soviet Union to secure agreement on NATO membership for a unified Germany. Bush’s diplomacy ensured a smooth reunification process. The episode demonstrated U.S. leadership in shaping post–Cold War Europe. It remains one of Bush’s most significant foreign-policy achievements.

treatiesNegotiated.title: NAFTA
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Clinton signed the North American Free Trade Agreement in 1993, creating a free-trade zone among the United States, Canada, and Mexico. The agreement reduced tariffs and expanded economic integration. Clinton argued that NAFTA would boost growth and competitiveness. Critics warned of job losses and outsourcing. NAFTA reshaped North American trade and became one of the most consequential economic agreements in U.S. history.

militaryActions.title: NATO Intervention in Kosovo
militaryActions.summary: Clinton authorized U.S. participation in NATO airstrikes against Yugoslavia in 1999 to stop ethnic cleansing in Kosovo. The campaign forced Serbian forces to withdraw and ended the humanitarian crisis. Clinton framed the intervention as a moral imperative to prevent genocide. Critics questioned the legality of bypassing the UN Security Council. The operation strengthened NATO’s post–Cold War role.

militaryActions.title: Operation Desert Fox
militaryActions.summary: In 1998, Clinton ordered airstrikes against Iraq after Saddam Hussein obstructed UN weapons inspections. The operation targeted military and security facilities. Clinton argued that the strikes were necessary to degrade Iraq’s ability to produce weapons of mass destruction. Critics questioned the timing and effectiveness. The operation reflected ongoing tensions with Iraq throughout the 1990s.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Support for Post-Soviet States
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Clinton expanded aid to former Soviet republics to support democratic transitions, nuclear disarmament, and economic reform. Programs helped dismantle nuclear weapons in Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan. Clinton viewed assistance as essential to preventing instability and proliferation. The aid strengthened U.S. influence in Eurasia. It also supported integration with Western institutions.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Dayton Accords
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Clinton brokered the Dayton Accords in 1995, ending the Bosnian War. The agreement created a federal structure for Bosnia and Herzegovina and deployed NATO peacekeepers. Clinton invested significant diplomatic effort in the negotiations. The accords ended one of the bloodiest conflicts in post–Cold War Europe. They remain a cornerstone of Balkan stability.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Normalization with Vietnam
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Clinton normalized diplomatic relations with Vietnam in 1995, ending decades of hostility after the Vietnam War. The decision followed progress on POW/MIA issues and economic reforms in Vietnam. Normalization expanded trade and strategic cooperation. It marked a major shift in U.S. policy toward Southeast Asia. The move helped integrate Vietnam into the global economy.

treatiesNegotiated.title: SORT Treaty (Moscow Treaty)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Bush signed the Strategic Offensive Reductions Treaty (SORT) with Russia in 2002. The treaty reduced deployed strategic nuclear warheads but lacked detailed verification measures. Bush viewed the agreement as a flexible approach to arms control. Critics argued that it was too vague. The treaty reflected improved U.S.–Russian relations after 9/11.

militaryActions.title: War in Afghanistan
militaryActions.summary: After the 9/11 attacks, Bush launched Operation Enduring Freedom to dismantle al-Qaeda and remove the Taliban from power. The invasion quickly toppled the Taliban regime but led to a prolonged insurgency. Bush framed the war as part of a global struggle against terrorism. The conflict became the longest war in U.S. history. It reshaped American foreign policy and military strategy.

militaryActions.title: Iraq War
militaryActions.summary: Bush ordered the invasion of Iraq in 2003, arguing that Saddam Hussein possessed weapons of mass destruction and posed a threat to global security. No such weapons were found. The invasion toppled Hussein but triggered a violent insurgency and sectarian conflict. The war became one of the most controversial U.S. military actions. It reshaped Middle Eastern geopolitics and U.S. global standing.

foreignAidPrograms.title: PEPFAR
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Bush launched the President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR) in 2003, providing billions of dollars to combat HIV/AIDS globally. The program focused on Africa and became one of the largest health initiatives in history. PEPFAR saved millions of lives and strengthened global health systems. It remains one of Bush’s most widely praised foreign-policy achievements.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Bush Doctrine
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: The Bush Doctrine asserted the right of the United States to launch preemptive strikes against emerging threats, particularly terrorism and rogue states. It emphasized unilateral action and the promotion of democracy. The doctrine guided U.S. policy in Afghanistan and Iraq. Critics argued that it justified unnecessary wars. Supporters viewed it as essential to national security after 9/11.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Global War on Terror
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Bush launched the Global War on Terror after 9/11, targeting terrorist networks worldwide. The campaign included military operations, intelligence cooperation, and homeland security reforms. Bush argued that terrorism required a global, long-term response. The policy reshaped U.S. alliances and military posture. It remains a defining feature of early 21st-century foreign policy.

treatiesNegotiated.title: New START Treaty
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Obama negotiated the New START Treaty with Russia in 2010, reducing deployed strategic nuclear warheads and launchers. The treaty included rigorous verification measures. Obama viewed arms control as essential to global stability. The agreement strengthened U.S.–Russian cooperation early in his presidency. It remains a cornerstone of nuclear arms control.

militaryActions.title: Operation Neptune Spear
militaryActions.summary: Obama authorized the raid that killed al-Qaeda leader Osama bin Laden in Pakistan in 2011. The operation was conducted by U.S. Navy SEALs and marked a major milestone in the fight against terrorism. Obama framed the mission as justice for the victims of 9/11. The raid improved Obama’s approval ratings and reshaped perceptions of U.S. counterterrorism capabilities.

militaryActions.title: Intervention in Libya
militaryActions.summary: Obama supported NATO airstrikes in Libya in 2011 to prevent mass atrocities during the uprising against Muammar Gaddafi. The intervention helped topple Gaddafi but left Libya unstable. Obama described the operation as a model of multilateral action. Critics argued that it lacked a plan for post-conflict reconstruction. The intervention remains controversial.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Global Health and Development Initiatives
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Obama expanded global health programs, including efforts to combat Ebola, malaria, and HIV/AIDS. His administration increased funding for development, education, and women’s rights. Obama viewed foreign aid as essential to global stability and U.S. leadership. These programs strengthened international partnerships. They also enhanced America’s soft power.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Iran Nuclear Deal (JCPOA)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Obama negotiated the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action with Iran and major world powers in 2015. The agreement limited Iran’s nuclear program in exchange for sanctions relief. Obama argued that the deal prevented Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons. Critics warned that it empowered Iran regionally. The agreement became one of the most debated foreign-policy achievements of the Obama era.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Pivot to Asia
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Obama announced a strategic 'pivot' or 'rebalance' to Asia, emphasizing economic, diplomatic, and military engagement in the Indo-Pacific. The policy aimed to counter China’s growing influence. It included expanded alliances, trade initiatives, and military deployments. The pivot reshaped U.S. strategy in the 21st century. It remains central to American foreign policy.

treatiesNegotiated.title: USMCA (United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement)
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Trump renegotiated NAFTA and replaced it with the USMCA in 2020. The agreement updated rules on labor standards, digital trade, automotive manufacturing, and intellectual property. Trump argued that USMCA corrected structural flaws in NAFTA and protected American workers and industries. The agreement received bipartisan support in Congress. It reshaped North American trade relations and became one of the most significant trade agreements of the 21st century.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Abraham Accords
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Trump brokered normalization agreements between Israel and several Arab states, including the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain, Sudan, and Morocco. These agreements, collectively known as the Abraham Accords, marked a major shift in Middle Eastern diplomacy. They established formal diplomatic relations, expanded trade, and increased security cooperation. Supporters hailed the accords as historic breakthroughs. Critics argued they bypassed the Israeli–Palestinian conflict. The accords reshaped regional alliances and strengthened U.S. influence in the Middle East.

militaryActions.title: Strike on Qasem Soleimani
militaryActions.summary: Trump ordered a drone strike in January 2020 that killed Iranian General Qasem Soleimani, leader of the Quds Force. The administration argued that Soleimani posed an imminent threat to U.S. personnel. Iran retaliated with missile strikes on U.S. bases in Iraq, injuring dozens of American troops. The strike escalated tensions between the United States and Iran and raised concerns about a broader conflict. It marked one of the most consequential military decisions of Trump’s presidency.

militaryActions.title: Airstrikes in Syria
militaryActions.summary: Trump ordered missile strikes on Syrian government targets in 2017 and 2018 in response to chemical weapons attacks by Bashar al-Assad’s regime. The strikes targeted chemical weapons facilities and were coordinated with U.S. allies. Trump framed the actions as necessary to enforce international norms against chemical warfare. Critics questioned the long-term strategy. The strikes signaled a willingness to use force while avoiding deeper involvement in Syria’s civil war.

foreignAidPrograms.title: COVID-19 Global Assistance
foreignAidPrograms.summary: During the COVID-19 pandemic, the Trump administration provided medical supplies, ventilators, and financial assistance to partner nations. The aid aimed to support global health systems and mitigate the spread of the virus. The administration also participated in some international scientific cooperation while declining to join certain multilateral initiatives. The pandemic reshaped global diplomacy and highlighted the role of health assistance in foreign policy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Withdrawal from the Iran Nuclear Deal (JCPOA)
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Trump withdrew the United States from the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action in 2018, arguing that the agreement was flawed and failed to address Iran’s ballistic missile program and regional activities. The administration reimposed sweeping sanctions on Iran under a 'maximum pressure' campaign. Critics warned that withdrawal increased nuclear risks. Supporters argued it restored U.S. leverage. The decision reshaped U.S.–Iran relations and Middle Eastern geopolitics.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Engagement with North Korea
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Trump held unprecedented summits with North Korean leader Kim Jong Un in Singapore (2018) and Hanoi (2019). The meetings aimed to negotiate denuclearization and reduce tensions on the Korean Peninsula. While the summits produced symbolic breakthroughs and temporary de-escalation, they did not result in a binding agreement. Critics argued that the diplomacy legitimized North Korea without securing concessions. Supporters viewed it as a bold attempt to break diplomatic deadlock.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Trade War with China
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Trump launched a major trade confrontation with China, imposing tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars’ worth of goods. The administration accused China of unfair trade practices, intellectual property theft, and currency manipulation. China retaliated with its own tariffs. The conflict disrupted global supply chains and reshaped U.S.–China economic relations. A 'Phase One' trade deal was signed in 2020, but many issues remained unresolved.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Withdrawal from International Agreements
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Trump withdrew the United States from several major international agreements, including the Paris Climate Accord, the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP), and the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty. The administration argued that these agreements disadvantaged the United States or were ineffective. Critics warned that withdrawals weakened global leadership and alliances. The decisions marked a shift toward unilateralism and skepticism of multilateral institutions.

treatiesNegotiated.title: AUKUS Security Pact
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Biden launched the AUKUS trilateral security partnership with Australia and the United Kingdom in 2021. The agreement included plans to help Australia acquire nuclear-powered submarines, expand joint military cooperation, and enhance technological collaboration in areas such as cyber, AI, and undersea capabilities. AUKUS was designed to counter China’s growing influence in the Indo-Pacific. The pact marked one of the most significant U.S. security agreements of the 21st century and reshaped regional strategic alignments.

treatiesNegotiated.title: Global Minimum Tax Agreement
treatiesNegotiated.summary: Biden helped negotiate a global agreement among more than 130 countries to establish a 15% minimum corporate tax rate. The deal aimed to prevent multinational corporations from shifting profits to low-tax jurisdictions. Although not a traditional treaty, it represented a major international economic accord. The agreement strengthened global tax cooperation and reflected Biden’s emphasis on multilateral economic governance.

militaryActions.title: Withdrawal from Afghanistan
militaryActions.summary: Biden completed the U.S. military withdrawal from Afghanistan in 2021, ending America’s longest war. The withdrawal followed the collapse of the Afghan government and rapid Taliban takeover. A chaotic evacuation from Kabul drew global attention and criticism. Biden argued that continued U.S. military presence could not change Afghanistan’s trajectory. The withdrawal reshaped U.S. foreign policy by shifting focus away from large-scale counterinsurgency operations.

militaryActions.title: Support for Ukraine (Non‑Combat Military Aid)
militaryActions.summary: Following Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, Biden authorized unprecedented levels of military assistance, including advanced weapons systems, intelligence support, and training. Although U.S. troops did not enter combat, the scale of aid made the United States Ukraine’s primary military backer. Biden framed the conflict as a defense of democracy against authoritarian aggression. The support reshaped NATO strategy and revived transatlantic unity.

foreignAidPrograms.title: COVID-19 Global Vaccine Distribution
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Biden launched one of the largest global vaccine donation programs in history, distributing hundreds of millions of COVID-19 vaccine doses to developing nations. The initiative aimed to curb the pandemic, strengthen global health systems, and counter vaccine diplomacy by rival powers. The program reflected Biden’s emphasis on multilateral cooperation and humanitarian leadership.

foreignAidPrograms.title: Economic and Humanitarian Aid to Ukraine
foreignAidPrograms.summary: Biden approved tens of billions of dollars in economic, humanitarian, and energy assistance to Ukraine following Russia’s invasion. Aid supported government operations, infrastructure repair, refugee assistance, and energy grid stabilization. The scale of support made Ukraine one of the largest recipients of U.S. foreign aid in modern history. The program reinforced U.S. leadership in the NATO alliance.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: Rejoining the Paris Climate Agreement
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: On his first day in office, Biden reentered the Paris Climate Agreement, reversing the previous administration’s withdrawal. The move signaled a renewed U.S. commitment to global climate cooperation. Biden pledged ambitious emissions reductions and expanded international climate financing. The decision restored U.S. leadership in global environmental diplomacy.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: NATO Expansion and Strengthening
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Biden supported the accession of Finland and Sweden to NATO, marking one of the largest expansions of the alliance in decades. The move was driven by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and shifting European security dynamics. Biden worked to unify NATO members around increased defense spending and coordinated sanctions. The expansion strengthened the alliance’s northern flank and reshaped European security.

majorForeignPolicyEvents.title: U.S.–China Strategic Competition
majorForeignPolicyEvents.summary: Biden intensified strategic competition with China while seeking to prevent conflict. His administration imposed export controls on advanced semiconductors, strengthened alliances in the Indo-Pacific, and increased naval operations in the South China Sea. Biden also pursued limited diplomatic engagement to stabilize relations. The approach combined deterrence with selective cooperation. It defined a central axis of 21st‑century U.S. foreign policy.
